text: The surgeon prepares for laparoscopic cholecystectomy and appendectomy on a six-year-old girl by inverting the umbilicus and making a small stab wound over its inferior crease using a scalpel. The Veress needle is carefully inserted, ensuring minimal exposure, and the abdominal wall is lifted to avoid muscle penetration. Tests such as irrigation, suction, and hanging drop are performed to confirm needle placement before insufflation. The insufflation pressure is set to 8 mmHg with a flow rate of 0.1 liters per minute per year of age, introducing 800 ml of gas into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon makes a small, smiling incision at the inferior crease of the umbilicus, dilating it to access the intestinal tract using a Scandinavian technique. A trocar is held like a pistol, with only four centimeters exposed, and inserted with a screwing motion. A telescope is introduced through the umbilical port, while a second port is placed epigastrically, just left of the falciform ligament. The trocar is inserted perpendicular, then angled towards the right after piercing the membranous part of the falciform ligament. A striker mini alligator is used percutaneously for the cholecystectomy and appendicectomy.

The surgeon uses a Striker mini-alligator to hold the gallbladder above the Hartmann's pouch, retracting it without a trocar. Adhesions are separated, and the posterior peritoneum is dissected with the harmonic scalpel. The cystic pedicle is exposed, and a posterior window is created using a Maryland dissector. The cystic artery is clipped with titanium clips, and the cystic duct is ligated with an extracorporeal Mishra's knot, then cut with scissors.

The surgeon uses entromedial and entrolateral traction with a percutaneous micro instrument to separate the gallbladder from the liver. The light cable is positioned at six o'clock, and the teflon or silicon jaw of the harmonic is kept towards the liver to prevent puncturing. Blunt dissection is employed to create a dissection plane, and the striker mini alligator is rearranged to minimize traction, facilitating the separation of the gallbladder fundus.

The surgeon inspects the area below the gallbladder fundus, ensuring the surgical field is clear and dry with no bleeding. The light cable is adjusted upwards for better visibility. The final part of the gallbladder is separated using a two-port technique, with one percutaneous instrument.

The surgeon uses a striker mini alligator to hold the neck of the gallbladder and carefully extracts it through the cannula. Ovum forceps are used to crush and remove multiple large gallstones from the gallbladder before complete removal. After gallbladder extraction, the ten-millimeter cannula is switched to the epigastric port, and the harmonic device is positioned in the umbilical port to perform an appendectomy. The silicon jaw of the harmonic is directed towards the appendix to prevent vibration transfer, ensuring complete separation at the base of the appendix. A pre-tagged Mishra's knot is applied using a knot pusher to secure the appendix before removal.

The surgeon uses the tip of the knot pusher over the tinea coli to secure the knot at the appendix junction, ensuring a complete appendectomy. The scissors are used to cut the knot, followed by partial cutting of the appendix with the harmonic device, which burns and seals the mucosa. The appendix is then rotated, caught, and transacted, ensuring the mucosa is sealed. The appendix is held longitudinally and pulled into the cannula using the striker mini alligator.

The appendix is pulled into the cannula and removed. The diagnostic laparoscopy reveals the ureter crossing the common iliac vessel, with visible anatomical structures including the sacral promontory, fimbrial end of the fallopian tube, external iliac artery and vein, ovary, uterus, uterosacral ligament, sigmoid colon, and bladder. The uterus is notably small, reflecting the patient's young age.

The surgeon identifies the medial amygdala ligament and the deep ring. The common iliac vessel and external iliac vessel on the left side are visible. The left ureter is observed with visible peristalsis. The small uterus and the triangle of doom are also identified.
